learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression used when realizing a connection between people

examples.english: We're from the same hometown, it's really fate.
We met by chance, it's really destiny.

learning_materials.notes: Used to express amazement at coincidental connections

learning_materials.english: The groom is the eldest son
learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression about family position in marriage context

examples.korean: 결혼하면 시부모님과 살아야 해요. 신랑이 장남이거든요.
examples.english: After marriage, we have to live with his parents. The groom is the eldest son.
Since the groom is the eldest son, there are many family events.

learning_materials.notes: Important in Korean family culture context

learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression of surprise at someone's maturity

examples.english: You're already a university student? You've become a full adult.

examples.korean: 혼자서 다 처리했네. 어른이 다 됐구나.
examples.english: You handled everything by yourself. You've become a full adult.

learning_materials.notes: Shows surprise at someone's growth or maturity

learning_materials.context_from_class: Grammar pattern showing realization or understanding

examples.english: Ah, so that's why you were late.
It's already 5 o'clock. Time flies.

learning_materials.notes: 구나 is casual, 군요 is polite. Shows sudden realization

learning_materials.context_from_class: Grammar pattern meaning 'immediately after doing something'

examples.english: I fell asleep as soon as I arrived home.
I left for a trip as soon as the exam ended.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of 하자마자 pattern with graduation

examples.english: I got a job immediately after graduation.
I plan to get married immediately after graduation.

learning_materials.english: Because I don't know what gift to give
learning_materials.context_from_class: Pattern for expressing uncertainty about what to do

examples.korean: 어떤 옷을 입어야 할지 몰라서 고민이에요.
examples.english: I'm worried because I don't know what clothes to wear.

examples.korean: 어디로 가야 할지 몰라서 길을 물어봤어요.
examples.english: I asked for directions because I didn't know where to go.

learning_materials.notes: Used with question words + 해야 할지 모르다

learning_materials.context_from_class: Grammar pattern with two meanings: simultaneous action or contrast

examples.english: I study while listening to music.
He has a lot of money but is stingy.

learning_materials.notes: Can show simultaneous actions or contradictory situations

learning_materials.english: Even though his conditions are good
learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of 면서 showing contrast in marriage context

examples.english: Even though his personality is good, why isn't he popular?
Even though his skills are good, he lacks confidence.

learning_materials.notes: Shows contradiction between expected and actual situation

learning_materials.english: Person who has a lot of (trait/behavior)
learning_materials.context_from_class: Suffix attached to words to describe people with certain characteristics

examples.english: Our child is really a troublemaker.
That person is a famous braggart.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of 쟁이 suffix meaning someone who talks a lot

examples.korean: 언니는 수다쟁이라서 전화하면 한 시간은 기본이에요.
examples.english: My sister is a chatterbox, so phone calls are at least an hour.
Thanks to my talkative friend, I'm never bored.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of 쟁이 suffix for someone who lies frequently

examples.korean: 그 사람 말은 믿지 마. 거짓말쟁이야.
examples.english: Don't believe what he says. He's a liar.
If you get a reputation as a liar, friends will leave you.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of 쟁이 suffix for someone who brags or exaggerates

examples.korean: 그는 허풍쟁이라서 말을 반만 믿어야 해.
examples.english: He's a braggart, so you should only believe half of what he says.
My braggart uncle started boasting again.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of 쟁이 suffix for someone who is easily scared

examples.english: I'm a coward so I can't watch scary movies.

examples.korean: 겁쟁이 동생은 혼자 화장실도 못 가요.
examples.english: My coward younger sibling can't even go to the bathroom alone.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of 쟁이 suffix for someone who is very stubborn

examples.english: Grandfather is stubborn so he doesn't listen.
It's hard to persuade my stubborn friend.

learning_materials.notes: Someone who won't change their mind easily

learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of 쟁이 suffix for someone who is greedy

examples.korean: 욕심쟁이라서 모든 걸 다 가지려고 해요.
examples.english: He's greedy so he tries to have everything.

examples.korean: 욕심쟁이 형은 음식을 혼자 다 먹어요.
examples.english: My greedy brother eats all the food by himself.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of 쟁이 suffix for someone who is lazy

examples.english: I'm lazy so I don't do homework.

examples.korean: 게으름쟁이 룸메이트 때문에 청소를 혼자 해요.
examples.english: Because of my lazy roommate, I clean alone.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of 쟁이 suffix for someone who causes trouble

examples.korean: 우리 반 말썽쟁이가 또 사고를 쳤어요.
examples.english: The troublemaker in our class caused trouble again.
When my troublemaker nephew comes home, it's chaotic.

learning_materials.notes: Often used for children who misbehave

learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of 쟁이 suffix for someone who is stylish

examples.english: My sister is stylish so she dresses well.
I asked my stylish friend for fashion advice.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of 쟁이 suffix for someone who works for salary

examples.english: I'm a salaried worker so I don't have much money.
Life as a salaried worker is tough.

learning_materials.english: That worked out perfectly/What perfect timing
learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression used when something happens at just the right time

examples.english: The rain stopped. That worked out perfectly.

examples.korean: 할인 행사를 하네요. 마침 잘 됐네요.
examples.english: They're having a sale. What perfect timing.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression showing agreement with a suggestion

examples.english: Shall we meet tomorrow? Yes, let's do that.
Shall we eat at this restaurant? That sounds good.

learning_materials.notes: Polite way to agree with plans

learning_materials.context_from_class: Pattern expressing tentative intention (about 50% certainty)

examples.korean: 영화 보러 갈까 하는데 같이 갈래요?
examples.english: I'm thinking of going to see a movie, want to come?
I'm thinking of sleeping early today, what do you think?

learning_materials.context_from_class: Pattern expressing stronger intention (about 80% certainty)

examples.english: I'm planning to study abroad next year, what do you think?

examples.korean: 이사갈 생각인데 좋은 동네 추천해 주세요.
examples.english: I'm planning to move, please recommend a good neighborhood.

learning_materials.english: I'm about to go/intending to go
learning_materials.context_from_class: Pattern expressing strong intention (about 98% certainty)

examples.korean: 지금 집에 가려는데 뭔가 필요한 거 있어요?
examples.english: I'm about to go home, do you need anything?
I'm about to go eat lunch, want to come together?

learning_materials.english: To worry about, to be troubled by
learning_materials.context_from_class: Verb meaning to think deeply about problems with some stress

examples.english: I'm worrying about career issues.
What are you worrying about so much?

learning_materials.english: To be in the middle of worrying/considering
learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression showing ongoing state of consideration

examples.english: I'm in the middle of considering which university to go to.
I'm considering whether to change jobs.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression for describing something that left the strongest impression

examples.english: What's the most impressive travel destination?

examples.korean: 가장 인상 깊은 영화를 추천해 주세요.
examples.english: Please recommend the most impressive movie.

learning_materials.notes: Can also say 가장 기억에 남는 (most memorable)

learning_materials.context_from_class: Verb for physical or personality resemblance

examples.english: The son really resembles his father.
Your personality resembles your mother's.

learning_materials.notes: Can use 을/를 or 와/과/하고/랑 particles

learning_materials.english: In my view/From what I can see
learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression for giving opinion based on observation

examples.korean: 제가 보기에는 그 사람이 거짓말하는 것 같아요.
examples.english: In my view, that person seems to be lying.

examples.korean: 제가 보기에는 이 방법이 더 좋을 것 같아요.
examples.english: From what I can see, this method seems better.

learning_materials.notes: Similar to 제 생각에는 but based more on observation

learning_materials.english: To be urgent, to be in a hurry
learning_materials.context_from_class: Adjective describing urgency or hurried personality

examples.korean: 급한 일이 생겨서 먼저 가야 해요.
examples.english: Something urgent came up so I have to leave first.
I have an impatient personality so I can't wait.

learning_materials.notes: Can describe situations or personality traits

learning_materials.context_from_class: Positive personality trait describing reliability

examples.korean: 그 사람은 성실해서 믿을 수 있어요.
examples.english: That person is sincere so you can trust them.
Diligent students get good grades.

learning_materials.notes: Highly valued personality trait in Korean culture

learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression describing someone with kindness and empathy

examples.korean: 따뜻한 마음을 가진 사람과 결혼하고 싶어요.
examples.english: I want to marry someone with a warm heart.
The teacher is someone with a warm heart.

learning_materials.notes: Common way to describe kind, caring people

learning_materials.english: To be meticulous, thorough, careful about details
learning_materials.context_from_class: Personality trait describing attention to detail

examples.korean: 꼼꼼한 성격이라서 실수를 잘 안 해요.
examples.english: I have a meticulous personality so I rarely make mistakes.
Please check it thoroughly.

learning_materials.notes: Generally positive trait in Korean culture

learning_materials.context_from_class: Personality trait opposite of 꼼꼼하다

examples.english: I have an absent-minded personality so I often lose things.
Don't be careless, do it step by step.

learning_materials.notes: Often used to describe someone who forgets things easily

learning_materials.context_from_class: Personality trait describing gentle kindness

examples.english: Please speak in a gentle voice.
That nurse is really kind.

learning_materials.notes: Often used to describe manner of speaking or behavior

learning_materials.context_from_class: Personality trait opposite of 상냥하다

examples.english: He looks blunt but has a warm heart.
He answered with an expressionless face.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Personality trait with 이다 ending

examples.english: Please participate actively.
I have an active personality so I have many friends.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Personality trait opposite of 적극적이다

examples.english: I want to change my passive personality.
Don't be so passive, express your opinion.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Personality trait describing social orientation

examples.english: Extroverted people are well-suited for sales jobs.
Thanks to my extroverted personality, I adapt quickly.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Personality trait opposite of 외향적이다

examples.korean: 내향적인 성격이라서 혼자 있는 시간이 필요해요.
examples.english: I have an introverted personality so I need alone time.
I'm introverted but comfortable with friends.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Personality trait describing social skills

examples.english: I have a sociable personality so I like parties.
Sociable people are good at networking.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Negative personality trait

examples.english: Selfish behavior hurts friends.

examples.korean: 너무 이기적이지 말고 다른 사람도 생각해.
examples.english: Don't be so selfish, think about others too.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Personality/attitude trait, especially mentioned about Korean fathers

examples.english: I grew up in a conservative family.
There are many people with conservative thoughts.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Personality trait describing careful decision-making

examples.english: It would be good to decide carefully.

examples.korean: 신중한 성격이라서 실수를 잘 안 해요.
examples.english: I have a cautious personality so I rarely make mistakes.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Personality trait about honesty

examples.english: You can trust an honest person.
Please tell me honestly.

learning_materials.notes: Uses 하다 ending, highly valued trait

learning_materials.english: To have a strong sense of responsibility
learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression describing someone who takes responsibility seriously

examples.korean: 책임감이 강한 사람이 리더가 되어야 해요.
examples.english: A person with a strong sense of responsibility should become the leader.

examples.korean: 책임감이 강해서 맡은 일을 끝까지 해요.
examples.english: I have a strong sense of responsibility so I finish what I'm assigned.

learning_materials.notes: Important trait in Korean work culture

learning_materials.context_from_class: Polite way to express personal opinion

examples.korean: 제 생각에는 이 방법이 더 좋을 것 같아요.
examples.english: In my opinion, this method seems better.
I think it's too expensive.

learning_materials.notes: Polite way to introduce personal opinions

learning_materials.english: What do you think about marriage?
learning_materials.context_from_class: Pattern for asking opinions about topics

examples.english: What do you think about Korean food?
What do you think about this problem?

learning_materials.english: What do you think of marriage?
learning_materials.context_from_class: Alternative pattern for asking opinions, using object particle

examples.english: What do you think of this movie?
What do you think of the new policy?

learning_materials.notes: Uses object particle 을/를 instead of 에 대해서

learning_materials.context_from_class: Ending used when referring to shared knowledge between speaker and listener

examples.english: They said it would rain today, you know.
That person has a good personality, you know.

learning_materials.notes: Implies 'you know this too' or common knowledge

learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression for discussing positive and negative aspects

examples.korean: 이 방법의 장점과 단점을 말해 주세요.
examples.english: Please tell me the advantages and disadvantages of this method.
Online classes have advantages and disadvantages.

learning_materials.notes: Same as 좋은 점과 나쁜 점

learning_materials.context_from_class: Alternative expression for pros and cons

examples.korean: 이 직업의 좋은 점과 나쁜 점을 알려 주세요.
examples.english: Please tell me the good points and bad points of this job.

examples.korean: 도시 생활의 좋은 점과 나쁜 점이 있어요.
examples.english: City life has good points and bad points.

learning_materials.english: Because it's not improving as much as I thought
learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression comparing expectation with reality

examples.english: It's not as difficult as I thought.
It's not as expensive as I thought.

learning_materials.english: To improve, to increase, to grow
learning_materials.context_from_class: Verb meaning to get better (skills) or increase (quantity)

examples.english: My Korean skills have improved a lot.
Prices have increased a lot recently.

learning_materials.notes: ㄹ irregular verb - 늘다 becomes 늡니다

learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression about making frequent errors

examples.english: I'm still a beginner so I make many mistakes.
I make more mistakes when I'm tired.

learning_materials.notes: Common way to describe error frequency

learning_materials.context_from_class: Explanation of why 4 is written as F in elevators

examples.english: They write F instead of 4th floor.
Many people dislike 4 because of the death character.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Explanation about homophones

examples.english: These two words have the same pronunciation.
The Chinese characters are different but the pronunciation is the same.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Pattern showing increasing degree

examples.english: The more I study, the more difficult it becomes.
The more I meet them, the more I like them.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Grammar pattern showing contrast with emphasis

examples.english: I went out even though it was raining.
The lights are on even though it's late.

learning_materials.notes: Stronger than just 는데, adds emphasis

learning_materials.context_from_class: Formal expression meaning despite, often used in writing

examples.english: I succeeded despite the difficulties.
We proceeded with the plan despite the opposition.

learning_materials.notes: More formal than 는데도, often used in writing

learning_materials.context_from_class: Pattern showing increasing degree with repetition

examples.english: The more you exercise, the healthier you become.
The more I think about it, the more complicated it gets.

learning_materials.notes: Often shortened to just 할수록 in speech

learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression for something that almost happened but didn't

examples.english: I almost fell down.
I almost was late but barely made it.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Example of 뻔하다 pattern

examples.english: I almost was late because of traffic.
I almost was late because my alarm didn't go off.

learning_materials.english: Something big almost happened/I almost got in big trouble
learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression for almost having a serious problem

examples.korean: 가스를 안 끄고 나갔어요. 큰일 날 뻔했어요.
examples.english: I left without turning off the gas. Something big almost happened.

examples.korean: 중요한 서류를 잃어버릴 뻔했어요. 큰일 날 뻔했어요.
examples.english: I almost lost important documents. I almost got in big trouble.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression for narrowly avoiding an accident

examples.korean: 갑자기 차가 나와서 사고가 날 뻔했어요.
examples.english: A car suddenly came out and an accident almost happened.
An accident almost happened on the icy road.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression for narrowly avoiding injury

examples.english: I tripped on the stairs and almost got hurt.
I almost got hurt near the construction site.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Adverb describing doing something in a hurry

examples.english: I prepared hurriedly so I missed something.
If you eat hurriedly, you'll get indigestion.

learning_materials.context_from_class: Expression of relief or gratitude for good outcome

examples.english: The rain stopped. That's fortunate.
The exam was easy. I'm glad.
